conflict_type: Conflicts between Keyword Constraints and Phrase Constraints
seed_instruction: Write a story about a house where every room leads to a different year in time.
org_constraint: The story must include the phrase "time bends to the will of the house" at least once.
new_constraint: The keyword "will" can not be used as a noun.
instruction: Write a story about a house where every room leads to a different year in time. The story must include the phrase "time bends to the will of the house" at least once. The keyword "will" can not be used as a noun.

Include the phrase "time bends to the will of the house" at least once.

    *Story:*
    The mansion sat on the hill like a gargoyle made of stone and shadow. Elias pushed the front door open, and the hinges groaned a low, metallic protest. He stepped into the foyer, where the air tasted of dust and ancient secrets.

    He turned into the first room on the left. Instantly, the dim light shifted into a warm, golden glow. A gramophone began to spin, scratching out a jazz melody from 1924. The walls were covered in velvet, and the scent of expensive tobacco hung in the air. He reached out to touch a chair, but his fingers met only a shimmering haze.

    He stepped through the door opposite the gramophone.

    The jazz vanished, replaced by the sharp, rhythmic clatter of a printing press. He was in a cramped, ink-stained workshop from 1888. Steam rose from a nearby boiler, and the air felt thick and oily. A man in a newsboy cap looked up, startled, before dissolving into a cloud of gray soot.

    Elias hurried through the next door.

    He found himself in a sterile, white corridor. The walls were made of a seamless, pearlescent material that hummed with electricity. A holographic display flickered in the corner, showing data streams from the year 2140. There were no windows, only glowing panels that pulsed like a heartbeat.

    He felt a strange vertigo, a pulling sensation in his marrow. The house was not just a structure; it was a labyrinth of moments. He moved from a medieval kitchen, where a fire crackled in a stone hearth, to a 1970s living room filled with orange plastic furniture and the smell of fried onions.

    Each room was a different era, a different life, a different death.

    He reached the center of the house, a grand rotunda where the ceiling vanished into a swirling vortex of gray mist. On the central pillar, carved into the wood in a script that seemed to move when he blinked, were the words:

    "time bends to the will of the house"

    Elias felt a cold shiver. He realized that he was not a guest; he was a specimen. The house was consuming his timeline, weaving his presence into its endless corridors.

    He knew he must leave. He will find the way out. He will not let the house claim his memories. He will run until the years stop shifting. The house will watch him, but he will escape.
